---
title: ""
author: ""
format: 
  beamer:
    theme: "CambridgeUS"
    colortheme: "default"
    keep-tex: true
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{amsmath}
  - \usepackage{amssymb}
  - \usepackage{tikz}
  - \usetikzlibrary{positioning, calc}
  - \date{\text{Jan - May, 2026}}
  - \let\olddate\date
  - \renewcommand{\date}[1]{}
  - \title[SIMA]{
      Statistical Inference and Multivariate Analysis (MA324)
    }
  - \subtitle{
      \textsc{Lecture Slides} \\
      Topic 01 \\
      Delta Method and Bootstrap
    }
---

## {.plain}

\titlepage

# Delta Method

## Introduction & Intuition

* **The Problem:** We know the asymptotic distribution of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$, but we want to know the asymptotic distribution of a continuous function $g(\hat{\theta})$.
* **The Solution:** The Delta Method uses a first-order Taylor series expansion to approximate the variance of the transformed estimator.
* **Taylor Expansion:** Around the true parameter $\theta$, the function $g(\hat{\theta})$ can be approximated as:
  $$g(\hat{\theta}) \approx g(\theta) + g'(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$$
* Rearranging terms, we get:
  $$g(\hat{\theta}) - g(\theta) \approx g'(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$$

## The Univariate Delta Method

::: {.block title="Theorem"}
Let $X_n$ be a sequence of random variables such that:
$$\sqrt{n}(X_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$
Suppose $g$ is a function such that its derivative $g'(\theta)$ exists and is non-zero. Then:
$$\sqrt{n}(g(X_n) - g(\theta)) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}\left(0, \sigma^2 \left[g'(\theta)\right]^2\right)$$
:::

\vspace{0.5cm}
**Implication:** The variance of the transformed estimator is approximately the original variance multiplied by the square of the derivative of the transformation evaluated at $\theta$.

## Proof Sketch: Univariate Delta Method

* By Taylor's Theorem and the Mean Value Theorem, we can write:
  $$g(X_n) = g(\theta) + g'(\tilde{\theta})(X_n - \theta)$$
  where $\tilde{\theta}$ lies strictly between $X_n$ and $\theta$.
* Rearranging and multiplying by $\sqrt{n}$:
  $$\sqrt{n}(g(X_n) - g(\theta)) = g'(\tilde{\theta})\sqrt{n}(X_n - \theta)$$
* Since $\sqrt{n}(X_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, we know $X_n \xrightarrow{p} \theta$. Thus, $\tilde{\theta} \xrightarrow{p} \theta$.
* By the Continuous Mapping Theorem, $g'(\tilde{\theta}) \xrightarrow{p} g'(\theta)$.
* Finally, applying **Slutsky's Theorem**:
  $$g'(\tilde{\theta})\sqrt{n}(X_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} g'(\theta) \cdot \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \equiv \mathcal{N}\left(0, \sigma^2 [g'(\theta)]^2\right)$$

## Example: Estimating Survival Probability

* Let lifetimes $T_1, \dots, T_n \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$. The MLE for the rate is $\hat{\lambda} = 1/\bar{T}$.
* From asymptotic likelihood theory, we know:
  $$\sqrt{n}(\hat{\lambda} - \lambda) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \lambda^2)$$
* We want the asymptotic distribution of the survival function at time $t_0$: $S(t_0) = \exp(-\lambda t_0)$.
* Let $g(\lambda) = \exp(-\lambda t_0)$. The derivative is $g'(\lambda) = -t_0 \exp(-\lambda t_0)$.
* Applying the Delta Method:
  $$\sqrt{n}\left(\exp(-\hat{\lambda} t_0) - \exp(-\lambda t_0)\right) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}\left(0, \lambda^2 \left[-t_0 \exp(-\lambda t_0)\right]^2\right)$$
* The asymptotic variance simplifies to $\lambda^2 t_0^2 \exp(-2\lambda t_0)$.

## Constructing Confidence Intervals

* We use a plug-in estimator for the standard error. Let $\widehat{\text{SE}}(g(\hat{\theta}))$ be:
  $$\widehat{\text{SE}}(g(\hat{\theta})) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{\sigma}^2 \left[g'(\hat{\theta})\right]^2}{n}}$$
* An approximate $(1 - \alpha)100\%$ confidence interval for $g(\theta)$ is:
  $$g(\hat{\theta}) \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}}(g(\hat{\theta}))$$
* Returning to our survival example, $\hat{S}(t_0) = \exp(-\hat{\lambda} t_0)$.
* The estimated standard error is:
  $$\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{S}(t_0)) = \frac{\hat{\lambda} t_0 \exp(-\hat{\lambda} t_0)}{\sqrt{n}}$$
* A $(1 - \alpha)100\%$ asymptotic CI for $S(t_0)$ is:
  $$\exp(-\hat{\lambda} t_0) \pm z_{\alpha/2} \left[ \frac{\hat{\lambda} t_0 \exp(-\hat{\lambda} t_0)}{\sqrt{n}} \right]$$

## Restricting Survival CIs to $[0, 1]$

* The standard linear CI for $\hat{S}(t_0)$ can yield bounds outside $[0, 1]$.
* **Solution:** Apply the Delta Method to a transformation that maps $(0, 1)$ to $(-\infty, \infty)$, such as the complementary log-log transformation: $g(S) = \ln(-\ln(S))$.
* The derivative is $g'(S) = \frac{1}{S \ln(S)}$. By the Delta Method:
  $$\widehat{\text{Var}}(\ln(-\ln(\hat{S}))) \approx \left[ \frac{1}{\hat{S} \ln(\hat{S})} \right]^2 \widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{S})$$
* Calculate the CI bounds for the transformed parameter: 
  $$[L_W, U_W] = \ln(-\ln(\hat{S})) \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}}(\ln(-\ln(\hat{S})))$$
* Transform back to get the $[0, 1]$-restricted CI for $S(t_0)$:
  $$\left[ \exp(-\exp(U_W)), \exp(-\exp(L_W)) \right]$$

## Simulation: Linear vs. Log-Log Bounds

```{r}
#| label: survival-ci-plot
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: 90%
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 8

# Simulate exponential survival data
set.seed(42)
n <- 25  
true_lambda <- 0.1
T_obs <- rexp(n, rate = true_lambda)
lambda_hat <- 1 / mean(T_obs)
t_seq <- seq(0, 40, length.out = 100)

# Estimated Survival Function
S_hat <- exp(-lambda_hat * t_seq)

# Standard Linear CI
z <- qnorm(0.975) 
se_linear <- (lambda_hat * t_seq * S_hat) / sqrt(n)
linear_lower <- S_hat - z * se_linear
linear_upper <- S_hat + z * se_linear

# Log-Log Transformed CI
se_loglog <- sqrt( (1 / (S_hat * log(S_hat)))^2 * (se_linear^2) )
loglog_lower <- exp(-exp( log(-log(S_hat)) + z * se_loglog )) 
loglog_upper <- exp(-exp( log(-log(S_hat)) - z * se_loglog )) 

# Plot
par(mar=c(4,4,1,1))
plot(t_seq, S_hat, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "black", 
     ylim = c(-0.15, 1.1), xlab = "Time (t)", ylab = "Survival Probability S(t)")
abline(h = c(0, 1), col = "gray", lty = 1)
lines(t_seq, linear_upper, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
lines(t_seq, linear_lower, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
lines(t_seq, loglog_upper, col = "blue", lty = 3, lwd = 2.5)
lines(t_seq, loglog_lower, col = "blue", lty = 3, lwd = 2.5)
legend("topright", legend = c("Estimated S(t)", "Linear CI (Violates 0 bound)", "Log-Log CI (Respects bounds)"), col = c("black", "red", "blue"), lty = c(1, 2, 3), lwd = 2, cex=0.8)
```

## The Second-Order Delta Method

* **Question:** What happens if $g'(\theta) = 0$? The first-order approximation collapses to $0$.
* If $g'(\theta) = 0$ but $g''(\theta) \neq 0$, we extend the Taylor series to the second order:
  $$g(X_n) \approx g(\theta) + g'(\theta)(X_n - \theta) + \frac{1}{2}g''(\theta)(X_n - \theta)^2$$
* Since $g'(\theta) = 0$, we multiply both sides by $n$ (instead of $\sqrt{n}$):
  $$n(g(X_n) - g(\theta)) \approx \frac{1}{2}g''(\theta) \left[\sqrt{n}(X_n - \theta)\right]^2$$
* Because $\sqrt{n}(X_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, its square converges to $\sigma^2 \chi^2_1$.
* **Result:** $n(g(X_n) - g(\theta)) \xrightarrow{d} \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 g''(\theta) \chi^2_1$.

## The Multivariate Delta Method

\begin{block}{Theorem}
Let $\mathbf{X}_n$ be a sequence of random vectors in $\mathbb{R}^k$ such that:
$$\sqrt{n}(\mathbf{X}_n - \boldsymbol{\theta}) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{0}, \Sigma)$$
Let $g: \mathbb{R}^k \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^m$ be a function whose Jacobian matrix $\nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ (of size $m \times k$) is evaluated at $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. Then:
$$\sqrt{n}(g(\mathbf{X}_n) - g(\boldsymbol{\theta})) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}_m\left(\mathbf{0}, \nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \Sigma \nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta})^T\right)$$
\end{block}

\vspace{0.2cm}
*Note: The matrix $\nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \Sigma \nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta})^T$ is the asymptotic covariance matrix of the transformed vector.*

# Bootstrap

## What is the Bootstrap?

* The **bootstrap** is a widely applicable and extremely powerful statistical tool.
* It is primarily used to quantify the uncertainty associated with a given estimator.
* **Common use cases:**
  * Estimating the standard errors of an estimator.
  * Constructing confidence intervals of a parameter.
  * Evaluating the performance of a complex machine learning model.
* **Core Idea:** To simulate drawing new samples from a population by resampling using original data.

## A Motivating Example: Asset Allocation

* Suppose we wish to invest a fixed sum of money in two financial assets that yield returns $X$ and $Y$.
* We will invest a fraction $\alpha$ in $X$ and $(1-\alpha)$ in $Y$.
* **Goal:** Choose $\alpha$ to minimize the total risk, or variance, of our investment.
* The variance of the return is:
$$
\text{Var}(\alpha X + (1-\alpha)Y) = \alpha^2 \sigma_X^2 + (1-\alpha)^2 \sigma_Y^2 + 2\alpha(1-\alpha)\sigma_{XY}
$$
* The value that minimizes this risk is:
$$
\alpha = \frac{\sigma_Y^2 - \sigma_{XY}}{\sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2 - 2\sigma_{XY}}
$$

## A Motivating Example: Asset Allocation

* $\sigma_X^2, \sigma_Y^2$, and $\sigma_{XY}$ are generally **unknown**.
* However, we can compute estimates ($\hat{\sigma}_X^2, \hat{\sigma}_Y^2, \hat{\sigma}_{XY}$).
* We then estimate the optimal allocation:
$$
\hat{\alpha} = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_Y^2 - \hat{\sigma}_{XY}}{\hat{\sigma}_X^2 + \hat{\sigma}_Y^2 - 2\hat{\sigma}_{XY}}
$$
* **Question:** How accurate is $\hat{\alpha}$ as an estimate of $\alpha$?
  <!-- What is its standard error? -->
* The bootstrap provides a simple way to answer.

## The Bootstrap Procedure

<!-- Instead of obtaining independent data sets from the true population (which is usually impossible), we obtain distinct data sets by repeatedly **sampling observations from the original data set with replacement**. -->
<!--  -->
<!-- **The Algorithm:** -->
<!--  -->
<!-- 1. Treat your original sample of size $n$ as the "population." -->
<!-- 2. Draw a new sample of size $n$ from this original sample *with replacement*. This is a **bootstrap data set** ($Z^{*1}$). -->
<!-- 3. Calculate the statistic of interest (e.g., $\hat{\alpha}^{*1}$) using this bootstrap data set. -->
<!-- 4. Repeat steps 2 and 3 a large number of times ($B$ times, e.g., $B = 1000$). -->
<!-- 5. You now have $B$ different bootstrap estimates: $\hat{\alpha}^{*1}, \hat{\alpha}^{*2}, \dots, \hat{\alpha}^{*B}$. -->
<!--  -->
<!-- ## Schematic of the Bootstrap Process -->

\begin{center}
\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{%
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    node distance=1.2cm and 0.5cm,
    box/.style={rectangle, draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!10, minimum width=2.5cm, minimum height=1cm},
    estimate/.style={rectangle, draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=orange!10, minimum width=2.5cm, minimum height=0.8cm},
    arrow/.style={->, thick, >=stealth}
]

% Original Data
\node[box, fill=green!10] (orig) {\textbf{Original Data} \\ $Z = (X_1, \dots, X_n)$};

% Bootstrap Samples
\node[box] (boot2) [below=of orig] {Bootstrap Sample 2 \\ $Z^{*2}$};
\node[box] (boot1) [left=of boot2, xshift=-0.5cm] {Bootstrap Sample 1 \\ $Z^{*1}$};
\node (dots) [right=of boot2, xshift=0.5cm] {\Large $\dots$};
\node[box] (bootB) [right=of dots, xshift=0.5cm] {Bootstrap Sample $B$ \\ $Z^{*B}$};

% Arrows from Original to Samples
\draw[arrow] (orig) -- (boot1.north);
\draw[arrow] (orig) -- (boot2.north);
\draw[arrow] (orig) -- (bootB.north);

% Estimates
\node[estimate] (est1) [below=of boot1] {Statistic $\hat{\alpha}^{*1}$};
\node[estimate] (est2) [below=of boot2] {Statistic $\hat{\alpha}^{*2}$};
\node (dots2) [below=of dots] {\Large $\dots$};
\node[estimate] (estB) [below=of bootB] {Statistic $\hat{\alpha}^{*B}$};

% Arrows from Samples to Estimates
\draw[arrow] (boot1) -- (est1);
\draw[arrow] (boot2) -- (est2);
\draw[arrow] (bootB) -- (estB);

% Calculation of center coordinate for correct alignment
\coordinate (row_center) at ($(est1.west)!0.5!(estB.east)$);

% Final Distribution
\node[box, fill=red!10, minimum width=14cm] (dist) at ([yshift=-2.5cm]row_center) {\textbf{Bootstrap Distribution} \\ Use to compute Standard Error $\text{SE}_B(\hat{\alpha})$, Confidence Intervals, etc.};

% Arrows from Estimates to Final Distribution
\draw[arrow] (est1) -- (dist.north -| est1.south);
\draw[arrow] (est2) -- (dist.north -| est2.south);
\draw[arrow] (estB) -- (dist.north -| estB.south);

\end{tikzpicture}
}
\end{center}

## Two Flavors of the Bootstrap

**1. Non-Parametric Bootstrap**

* Makes **no assumptions** about the underlying population distribution.
* Resamples directly from the observed data (the empirical distribution).
* **Use when:** The true distribution is unknown, complex, or you cannot confidently commit to a parametric model.

**2. Parametric Bootstrap**

* Assumes the data comes from a **known parametric family** of distributions (e.g., Normal, Exponential, Poisson).
* Estimates the parameters from the data, then draws new samples from the fitted theoretical distribution.
* **Use when:** You have strong theoretical or empirical reasons to assume a specific probability distribution.

## Non-Parametric Bootstrap: Example

**Goal:** Estimate distribution of the sample median.  
**Observed Data:** A sample of $n=5$: $X = \{3, 8, 2, 10, 5\}$.  
**Observed Sample Median:** $5$

**The Procedure:**

1. **Resample with replacement** directly from $X$ to create $B$ bootstrap datasets of size $n=5$.
   * Sample $X^{*1} = \{3, 3, 10, 2, 8\} \implies \text{Median}^{*1} = 3$
   * Sample $X^{*2} = \{8, 8, 5, 2, 5\} \implies \text{Median}^{*2} = 5$
   * Sample $X^{*3} = \{10, 10, 10, 3, 2\} \implies \text{Median}^{*3} = 10$
2. **Calculate:** Compute the median for all $B$ (e.g., $B=1000$) samples. Draw
   histogram of bootstrap estimates.
<!-- 3. **Evaluate:** Find the standard deviation of these $1000$ medians to get the standard error, $\text{SE}_B(\text{Median})$. -->

## Algorithm: The Non-Parametric Bootstrap

**Input:**

* Observed sample data: $X = (X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$
* Statistic of interest: $\hat{\theta} = T(X)$
* Number of bootstrap replicates: $B$ (typically $\ge 1000$)

**Procedure:**

1. **For** $b = 1$ **to** $B$ **do:**
   * **Step 1 (Resample):** Draw a random sample of size $n$ from $X$ **with replacement** to form the bootstrap sample $X^{*b} = (X_1^{*b}, X_2^{*b}, \dots, X_n^{*b})$.
   * **Step 2 (Calculate):** Evaluate the statistic on this bootstrap sample:
     $$
     \hat{\theta}^{*b} = T(X^{*b})
     $$
2. **End For**

**Output:** $\left\{ \hat{\theta}^{*1}, \hat{\theta}^{*2}, \dots, \hat{\theta}^{*B} \right\}$

## Parametric Bootstrap: Example

**Goal:** Estimate the standard error of the mean time to failure.  
**Observed Data:** A sample of $n=5$: $x = \{41.1, 14.7, 16.3, 5.6, 22.3\}$.  
**Probability Model Assumption:** The lifetime $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$.

**The Procedure:**

1. **Estimate Parameter:** Calculate the maximum likelihood estimate for the rate parameter from the original data, $\hat{\lambda} = 0.05$.
2. **Simulate from Model:** Generate $B$ new datasets of size $n=5$ directly from $\text{Exp}(\lambda = 0.05)$ using a random number generator.
   * Sample $X^{*1}: \left\{ 5.39,54.23, 16.40, 38.47,  3.89\right\} \implies \bar{X}^{*1} = 23.67$
   * Sample $X^{*2}: \left\{ 20.88, 7.14, 62.54, 62.50, 5.98 \right\} \implies \bar{X}^{*2} = 31.81$
   * Sample $X^{*3}: \left\{ 1.40, 45.69,  2.19, 11.02, 41.20 \right\} \implies \bar{X}^{*2} = 20.30$
3. **Calculate:** Compute the mean for all $B$ (e.g., $B=1000$) samples. Draw
   histogram  of bootstrap estimates.
<!-- 3. **Calculate & Evaluate:** Compute the sample mean for each simulated dataset, then find the standard deviation to get $\text{SE}_B(\bar{X})$. -->

## Algorithm: The Parametric Bootstrap

**Input:**

* Observed sample data: $X = (X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$
* Assumed parametric probability model: $F_\theta$ 
  <!-- (e.g., $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$) -->
* Statistic of interest: $\hat{\alpha} = T(X)$
* Number of bootstrap replicates: $B$ (typically $\ge 1000$)

## Algorithm: The Parametric Bootstrap (Cont.)

**Procedure:**

1. **Estimate:** Calculate the parameter estimate $\hat{\theta}$ from the original sample $X$ (e.g., via Maximum Likelihood Estimation).
2. **For** $b = 1$ **to** $B$ **do:**
   * **Step 1 (Simulate):** Generate a random sample of size $n$ directly from the fitted distribution $F_{\hat{\theta}}$ to form the bootstrap sample $X^{*b} = (X_1^{*b}, X_2^{*b}, \dots, X_n^{*b})$.
   * **Step 2 (Calculate):** Evaluate the statistic on this simulated bootstrap sample:
     $$
     \hat{\theta}^{*b} = T(X^{*b})
     $$
3. **End For**

**Output:** $\left\{ \hat{\theta}^{*1}, \hat{\theta}^{*2}, \dots, \hat{\theta}^{*B} \right\}$

## Estimating the Standard Error

* Once we have our $B$ bootstrap estimates, we can analyze their distribution.
* Bootstrap estimates : $\hat\alpha^{*1},\, \hat\alpha^{*2},\, \ldots,\, \hat\alpha^{*B}$.
* The **bootstrap standard error** is simply the sample standard deviation of the bootstrap estimates:
$$
\text{SE}_B(\hat{\alpha}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{i=1}^B \left( \hat{\alpha}^{*i} - \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i'=1}^B \hat{\alpha}^{*i'} \right)^2}
$$
* This serves as an estimate of the standard error of $\hat{\alpha}$ estimated from the original data set.

## Bootstrap Confidence Intervals: Overview

* While the Bootstrap Standard Error ($\text{SE}_B$) provides a measure of spread, we often want a formal Confidence Interval (CI) for our parameter $\theta$.
* If the estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is strictly normally distributed, we could simply use the Normal Approximation:
$$
\hat{\theta} \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE}_B(\hat{\theta})
$$
* However, the primary power of the bootstrap is that we **do not need to rely on the normality assumption**.
* We can compute intervals directly from the empirical bootstrap distribution.

## The Percentile Bootstrap CI

* The **Percentile Interval** is the most intuitive approach.
* We simply use the empirical quantiles of our computed bootstrap distribution.
* Let $\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)}$ and $\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)}$ be the $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$ quantiles of the $B$ bootstrap estimates.
* The $100(1-\alpha)\%$ Percentile Bootstrap CI is:
$$
\left[ \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)}, \ \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)} \right]
$$
* **Advantage:** Very simple to understand and compute; respects the natural mathematical bounds of the parameter.

## The Basic (Pivotal) Bootstrap CI

* The **Basic Interval** relies on approximating the distribution of the pivotal quantity $\delta = \hat{\theta} - \theta$.
* We approximate the true distribution of $R$ using the bootstrap distribution of $\delta^* = \hat{\theta}^* - \hat{\theta}$.
* Substituting the bootstrap quantiles and rearranging to isolate $\theta$ yields the Basic CI:
$$
\left[ 2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)}, \ 2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)} \right]
$$
<!-- * *Note:* The quantiles appear "reversed" compared to the percentile method because we are subtracting the bootstrap distribution from the point estimate. -->

<!-- ## 3. The BCa Confidence Interval -->
<!--  -->
<!-- * The **Bias-Corrected and Accelerated (BCa)** interval is widely considered the "gold standard" for bootstrap CIs. -->
<!-- * It adjusts the Percentile Method for two potential issues: -->
<!--   1.  **Bias:** The median of the bootstrap estimates may not exactly equal the original point estimate $\hat{\theta}$. -->
<!--   2.  **Acceleration:** The standard error of $\hat{\theta}$ might vary depending on the true value of $\theta$. -->
<!-- * The formula computes adjusted quantiles using a bias-correction parameter $\hat{z}_0$ and an acceleration parameter $\hat{a}$ (often computed via jackknife). -->
<!-- * **When to use:** Preferred for robust practical applications. -->

<!-- ## Visualizing the Bootstrap Distribution -->
<!--  -->
<!-- * After generating $B = 1000$ bootstrap estimates of $\hat{\alpha}$, we can plot their overall shape. -->
<!-- * Notice that the distribution looks approximately normal, centered around our original estimate. -->
<!--  -->
<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- #| label: boot-hist -->
<!-- #| echo: false -->
<!-- #| fig-align: center -->
<!-- #| fig-width: 6 -->
<!-- #| fig-height: 3 -->
<!-- library(ISLR2) -->
<!-- library(boot) -->
<!--  -->
<!-- # 1. Define the function to calculate alpha -->
<!-- alpha.fn <- function(data, index) { -->
<!--   X <- data$X[index] -->
<!--   Y <- data$Y[index] -->
<!--   return((var(Y) - cov(X, Y)) /  -->
<!--          (var(X) + var(Y) - 2 * cov(X, Y))) -->
<!-- } -->
<!--  -->
<!-- # 2. Run the bootstrap with B = 1000 -->
<!-- set.seed(1) -->
<!-- boot.out <- boot(Portfolio, alpha.fn, R = 1000) -->
<!-- hist(boot.out$t, breaks = 30, col = "lightblue",  -->
<!--      main = "", -->
<!--      xlab = "Bootstrap Estimates",  -->
<!--      border = "white", freq = FALSE) -->
<!-- curve(dnorm(x, mean(boot.out$t), sd(boot.out$t)),  -->
<!--       add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2) -->
<!-- ``` -->
<!--  -->
<!-- ## Visualizing the Spread (Boxplot) -->
<!--  -->
<!-- * A boxplot provides a concise summary of the same distribution, highlighting the median, quartiles, and any potential outliers. -->
<!-- * The interquartile range is closely related to $\text{SE}_B(\hat{\alpha})$. -->
<!--  -->
<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- #| label: boot-box -->
<!-- #| echo: false -->
<!-- #| fig-align: center -->
<!-- #| fig-width: 4 -->
<!-- #| fig-height: 4 -->
<!--  -->
<!-- boxplot(boot.out$t, col = "orange",  -->
<!--         main = "", -->
<!--         ylab = "Bootstrap Estimates") -->
<!-- ``` -->
<!--  -->
<!--  -->
<!-- ## Summary of the Bootstrap -->
<!--  -->
<!-- * The bootstrap allows us to compute standard errors and confidence intervals for estimators where mathematical derivations are impossible or complex. -->
<!-- * It relies on the assumption that the original sample is a good representation of the underlying population. -->
<!-- * **Key Takeaway:** We mimic the process of obtaining new data sets from the population by obtaining new data sets from our original sample. -->

---

## An empirical bootstrap confidence interval

* The [sample data:]{style="color: red;"} 30, 37, 36, 43, 42, 43, 43, 46, 41, 42
* Problem: [Estimate the mean $\mu$]{style="color: red;"} of the underlying distribution and give an [80% bootstrap confidence interval.]{style="color: red;"}
* The sample mean is $\bar{x} = 40.3$. We [use this as an estimate]{style="color: red;"} of the true mean $\mu$ of the underlying distribution. 
* To make the confidence interval we need to know how much the [distribution of $\bar{x}$ varies around $\mu$.]{style="color: red;"}
* In fact, we want to know the [distribution of $\delta = \bar{x} - \mu$]{style="color: red;"}.
* It says that we can approximate it by the distribution of $\delta^* = \bar{x}^* - \bar{x}$ where $\bar{x}^*$ is the mean of an empirical bootstrap sample.


---

## An empirical bootstrap confidence interval (cont.)

**Bootstrap Samples (20 iterations):**

| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 43| 36| 46| 30| 43| 43| 43| 37| 42| 42| 43| 37| 36| 42| 43| 43| 42| 43| 42| 43|
| 43| 41| 37| 37| 43| 43| 46| 36| 41| 43| 43| 42| 41| 43| 46| 36| 43| 43| 43| 42|
| 42| 43| 37| 43| 46| 37| 36| 41| 36| 43| 41| 36| 37| 30| 46| 46| 42| 36| 36| 43|
| 37| 42| 43| 41| 41| 42| 36| 42| 42| 43| 42| 43| 41| 43| 36| 43| 43| 41| 42| 46|
| 42| 36| 43| 43| 42| 37| 42| 42| 42| 46| 30| 43| 36| 43| 43| 42| 37| 36| 42| 30|
| 36| 36| 42| 42| 36| 36| 43| 41| 30| 42| 37| 43| 41| 41| 43| 43| 42| 46| 43| 37|
| 43| 37| 41| 43| 41| 42| 43| 46| 46| 36| 43| 42| 43| 30| 41| 46| 43| 46| 30| 43|
| 41| 42| 30| 42| 37| 43| 43| 42| 43| 43| 46| 43| 30| 42| 30| 42| 30| 43| 43| 42|
| 46| 42| 42| 43| 41| 42| 30| 37| 30| 42| 43| 42| 43| 37| 37| 37| 42| 43| 43| 46|
| 42| 43| 43| 41| 42| 36| 43| 30| 37| 43| 42| 43| 41| 36| 37| 41| 43| 42| 43| 43|


## An empirical bootstrap confidence interval (cont.)

* Next we compute $\delta^* = \bar{x}^* - \bar{x}$ for each bootstrap sample and
  sort them smallest to biggest:
  
| | | | | | | | | | |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| -1.6 | -1.4 | -1.4 | -0.9 | -0.5 | -0.2 | -0.1 | 0.1 | 0.2 | 0.2 |
| 0.4 | 0.4 | 0.7 | 0.9 | 1.1 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.6 | 1.6 | 2.0 |

* 90th percentile -> 18th element -> $1.6$
* 10th percentile -> 2nd element -> $-1.4$
* Therefore our [bootstrap 80% confidence interval]{style="color: red;"} for $\mu$ is
$$[\bar{x} -\delta_{0.1}^*, \bar{x} - \delta_{0.9}^*] = [40.3 - 1.6, 40.3 + 1.4] = [38.7, 41.7]$$
* In this example we only generated 20 bootstrap samples so they would fit on the page.

## References

::: {.nonincremental}

* **James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R.** (2013). *An Introduction to Statistical Learning with Applications in R*. Springer.


* **StatMethods.** *Bootstrapping: Quick-R*. [statmethods.net/advstats/bootstrapping.html](https://www.statmethods.net/advstats/bootstrapping.html)

:::

---
